annotations.text: Did this patient have a community-acquired pneumonia? The increased frequency of community-acquired pneumonias is well recognized in patients infected with HIV.

Since  *Pneumocystis carinii* is the most common single pathogen causing pneumonia in patients with AIDS, occurring in 80 percent of such patients during the course of their illness, I have to include it in the differential diagnosis of this patient's disease

The nonproductive cough, dyspnea, and fever, the widened arterialalveolar gradient, and the radiographic pattern of diffuse interstitial pulmonary infiltrates are all consistent with pneumonia caused by  *P. carinii.
Several features of the case, however, are inconsistent with this diagnosis. Crackles and wheezing are rarely heard on examination of the chest in patients with pneumocystis pneumonia.
Another opportunistic pathogen frequently encountered in association with  *P. carinii* in lung tissue of patients with AIDS is cytomegalovirus

Although cytomegalovirus is frequently recovered from respiratory tract secretions or on histologic examination of lung specimens of patients with AIDS, there have been only a few reports of cytomegalovirus pneumonitis without evidence of a concomitant pathogen.

There are well-documented cases of pneumonia in the AIDS population that has been attributed to  *Toxoplasma gondii* and lung involvement with this unicellular protozoan parasite has frequently been found in patients who have died of toxoplasmosis

Although probably uncommon, pulmonary toxoplasmosis should be considered in this patient. Fever and dyspnea are frequent in patients with pulmonary toxoplasmosis, but cough and sputum production are usually absent.

*Strongyloides stercoralis* is another interesting protozoan. Infection with  *S. stercoralis* is among the opportunistic infections considered indicative of an underlying cell-mediated immunodeficiency in patients with AIDS

The symptoms include fever, with rise of the temperature as high as 40°C, headache, general malaise with weight loss, nausea and vomiting, dyspnea, cough, and wheezing. Infiltrates demonstrable on radiographs of the chest may be either diffuse or involve mainly the lower lobes.
In trying to narrow the differential diagnosis I shall now focus on the radiographic finding of pulmonary reticulonodular infiltrates with questionable hilar lymphadenopathy.

The diseases with which an association of lung infiltrates and intrathoracic lymphadenopathy can be seen with any frequency in the AIDS population include mainly mycobacterial infections, fungal infections, Kaposi's sarcoma, and lymphoproliferative disorders.
Kaposi's sarcoma is the most frequently encountered tumor in persons with AIDS, affecting about 35 to 48 percent of them.

The disease is often shown to be diffuse on radiographs of the chest, and two patterns that seem to correlate with the histologic findings have been described — poorly marginated, nodular-appearing densities and linear interstitial densities.
Another neoplasm process that is associated with pulmonary infiltrates and hilar lymphadenopathy in patients with AIDS is non-Hodgkin's lymphoma.
Most of the lymphomas are of B-cell type and are aggressive tumors. They are usually not extranodal but do very rarely affect the lungs.

I come to the possibility of mycobacterial disease in this patient. It is believed that because of the profound dysfunction in T-cell activity in patients with HIV infection, mycobacterial disease in patients with AIDS presents with particular features.

Not only is the disease more frequent and more often disseminated in these patients than in the general population, but also granulomas tend to be poorly formed or nonexistent. The tuberculin skin-test reaction is frequently negative.
Disease due to  *Mycobacterium avium–intracellulare* (also called  *M. avium* complex) is known to be prevalent among patients with AID
As opposed to tuberculosis, infection with  *M. avium–intracellulare* occurs late in HIV disease, when other AIDS-related complications are often present

I shall now discuss fungal infections that are frequently encountered in patients with AIDS and that usually present with diffuse interstitial infiltrates and occasionally present with hilar or mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

Although disseminated candidiasis was found at autopsy in 12 of 56 patients, 10 of whom had pulmonary involvement, it is generally agreed that pulmonary candidiasis is a late or terminal manifestation of HIV infection and often coexists with other AIDS-related opportunistic infections.

I am finally led to consider infection with  *Histoplasma capsulatum* in this patient. Histoplasmosis has been reported in patients with AIDS from areas where this infection is endemic and from areas where it is not

Patients from areas where it is not endemic almost uniformly report travel to areas of endemic infection, which include the Caribbean islands, Central and South America, and the Ohio and Mississippi River valleys in the United States.

Although I cannot rule out miliary tuberculosis on the basis of the findings, I favor progressive disseminated histoplasmosis as the principal diagnosis in this case because of the history of recent previous residence in an area where the infection is endemic and the relatively slow evolution of the disease, with minimal respiratory tract symptoms.
